coredata.dc:title: Intracellular pH-propelled assembly of smart carbon nanodots and selective photothermal therapy for cancer cells
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Xue
Shen, Yanting
Xu, Shuping
Yue, Jing
Guo, Qiong
Huang, Dianshuai
Yang, Bai
Shi, Wei
Liang, Chongyang
Xu, Weiqing
coredata.dc:description: A kind of smart carbon nanodots (CNDs) with the pH response feature was prepared by the one-pot hydrothermal treatment of citric acid and dicyandiamide, which was used for the differentiation of cancer/normal cells and the selective photothermal therapy (PTT) of cancer cells. When the smart CNDs were cultured with cells, they were highly internalized in the lysosomes of cells. Since the small-sized CNDs (about 5 nm) tends to form aggregation (as large as about 20 nm or even larger) under an acid condition (pH = 4.7) due to the electrostatic attraction produced by the surface protonation, relatively severer aggregation of the CNDs were observed in liver cancer cells (HepG2 cells) relative to normal ones (LO2 cells) due to a relative lower pH in the lysosomes of HepG2 cells, which endows them a new strong absorption band at longer wavelengths (450−900 nm) and a higher photothermal conversion efficiency (42.13 %), benefiting to differentiated PTT. The flow cytometric data indicates strong photothermal ablation (8 min, 509.6 mW/cm2) for cancer cells with the assistance of these smart CNDs achieves 82 % death rate of cancer cells, while much less damage is observed on the normal cells (6.35 %). To the best of our knowledge, this is the first report about CNDs for selective PTT owing to their intrinsic property without the aid of any other targeting ligands. These smart CNDs are also available for other acid-responsive sensing systems, and this study inspires us in the synthesis of near-infrared featured carbon materials.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon nanodots
Assembly
Supra-carbon nanodots
Selective photothermal therapy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of smart CNDs and supra-CNDs
Characterizations of smart CNDs and supra-CNDs
Optimization of pH response of smart CNDs
Internalization of smart CNDs in cells
Tracking positions of smart CNDs in cells
PTT effect with the smart CNDs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 21573087 , 21573092 and 21873039 ). Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) A diagram of the formation of supra-CNDs. TEM images of the smart CNDs (b), supra-CNDs (c) and an enlarged image of the supra-CNDs (d). (e) UV–vis-NIR absorption of the smart CNDs and the supra-CNDs (1.5 mg/mL). (g) Fluorescence spectra of the smart CNDs and the supra-CNDs (1.5 mg/mL). (f) and (h) Photos of the smart CNDs and the supra-CNDs under the bright light and a 365 nm lamp.
(a) Infrared spectra of the smart CNDs (black) and the supra-CNDs (red). (b–d) are the C1s, N1s and O1s spectra of the supra-CND solid achieved by freeze-drying proccessing.

(a) The Uv–vis-NIR absorption spectra of the smart CND solutions (2.4 mg/mL) after their pH was tuned from 10.2 to lower values (6.8-4.7) and then aged at 37 °C for 24 h. Photo of the smart CNDs contained solutions with different pH values under a bright light (b) and a 365 nm lamp (c).

(a) Temperature evolutions of the supra-CNDs aqueous solution with different concentrations under the 650 nm light irradiation (407.6 mW/cm2). (b) Photothermal effect of the supra-CNDs aqueous solution (5 mg/mL) under 650 nm light irradiation with the power density of 118.5, 189.8, and 407.6 mW/cm2, respectively. (c) Smart CNDs (5.0 mg/mL), supra-CNDs (5.0 mg/mL) and pure water under the 650 nm irradiation (407.6 mW/cm2). (d) Photothermal effect of the supra-CND aqueous solution (2.5 mg/mL) under 650 nm light irradiation with the power density of 407.6 mW/cm2. The laser was shut off after at 930 s. (e) The time constant for heat transfer from the system was determined to be τs = 521.4 s by applying the linear time data from the cooling period (after 930 s) versus negative natural logarithm of the driving force temperature obtained from the cooling stage of (d).
Confocal fluorescent images of HepG2 and LO2 cells incubated with different concentrations of the smart CNDs for 24 h. Scale bar is 200 μm.
The quantum yields of the CNDs at different pH values.

head.ce:title: Intracellular pH-propelled assembly of smart carbon nanodots and selective photothermal therapy for cancer cells

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Supramolecular Structure and Materials, Institute of Theoretical Chemistry, College of Chemistry, Jilin University, Changchun 130012, People’s Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Supramolecular Structure and Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Lab for Molecular Enzymology & Engineering of Ministry of Education, Jilin University, Changchun 130012, People’s Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Lab for Molecular Enzymology & Engineering of Ministry of Education

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: b Key Lab for Molecular Enzymology & Engineering of Ministry of Education, Jilin University, Changchun 130012, People’s Republic of China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Frontier Medical Science, Jilin University, Changchun 130021, People’s Republic of China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: c Institute of Frontier Medical Science, Jilin University, Changchun 130021, People’s Republic of China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A kind of smart carbon nanodots (CNDs) with the pH response feature was prepared by the one-pot hydrothermal treatment of citric acid and dicyandiamide, which was used for the differentiation of cancer/normal cells and the selective photothermal therapy (PTT) of cancer cells. When the smart CNDs were cultured with cells, they were highly internalized in the lysosomes of cells. Since the small-sized CNDs (about 5 nm) tends to form aggregation (as large as about 20 nm or even larger) under an acid condition (pH = 4.7) due to the electrostatic attraction produced by the surface protonation, relatively severer aggregation of the CNDs were observed in liver cancer cells (HepG2 cells) relative to normal ones (LO2 cells) due to a relative lower pH in the lysosomes of HepG2 cells, which endows them a new strong absorption band at longer wavelengths (450−900 nm) and a higher photothermal conversion efficiency (42.13 %), benefiting to differentiated PTT. The flow cytometric data indicates strong photothermal ablation (8 min, 509.6 mW/cm2) for cancer cells with the assistance of these smart CNDs achieves 82 % death rate of cancer cells, while much less damage is observed on the normal cells (6.35 %). To the best of our knowledge, this is the first report about CNDs for selective PTT owing to their intrinsic property without the aid of any other targeting ligands. These smart CNDs are also available for other acid-responsive sensing systems, and this study inspires us in the synthesis of near-infrared featured carbon materials.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon nanodots (CNDs) with various optical, photoelectronic, and well biocompatible properties have attracted more attentions since they were discovered in 2004 [1]. These new-born nanomaterials are easy to fabricate, and the synthetic materials for CNDs are diverse and easy to find. CNDs are usually as small as several nanometres, and their radiative colours have been proved to agree on the quantum state that is size-dependent [2]. Beside outstanding optical properties including high optical brightness, photostability and excitation/size-dependent emission as well, CNDs also feature many unique and excellent properties, such as good dispersibility in water, low toxicity, and excellent biocompatibility. These merits make CNDs promising in advancing biological applications and they have already become another star bio nanomaterial after quantum dots (QDs) [3–19]. Nowadays, CNDs have been used for bioimaging contrast agents [4–9], nanodrug carriers [11–13], and intracellular biosensors [16].

Photothermal therapy (PTT) is a new emerging cancer therapeutics that employs photosensitive agents to generate hyperthermia under light irradiation, which can inhibit the metastasis of tumours and kill cancer cells. Inorganic nanomaterials including gold nanoparticles, rare earth-doped nanoparticles and semiconductor QDs are widely studied as PTT agents due to their excellent performances in photothermal conversion [20]. However, they usually show relatively larger biotoxicity and degradation-resistance, which limits their medical applications [21]. Compared with metallic nanomaterials and QDs, CNDs show better biocompatibility and excellent optical properties. Therefore, many attempts have been devoted to adopt CNDs as a new category of PTT agents [22–26]. The reported CNDs usually have an intrinsic absorption band in the wavelength range of 300−400 nm [27–31], which overlaps the self-absorption of biotissues. They also share a low molar absorptivity index, which causes light utilization inefficiency and is disadvantageous to PTT. Moreover, owing to a small size of CNDs (∼6 nm), it is hard to guarantee a sufficiently long residence time in tumours. Therefore, efforts have been endeavoured to develop new CNDs with strong photothermal responsibility, deeper tissue penetration depth and longer retention time.

As we all know, the aggregation state of nanoparticles can enlarge the adsorption cross section, and provides an additional absorption band in longer wavelengths as well. Previous studies have proved that the humidity-driven CND assemblies (noted as supra-CNDs) own a high photothermal conversion efficiency to 53.2 % due to the new generated peak in the red and near-infrared (NIR) range [32]. In the recent report, these supra-CNDs have been successfully applied for photothermal therapy [22]. Inspired by the supra-CNDs [32], we designed a novel kind of smart CNDs (Fig. 1 a) that show the pH-propelled self-aggregation feature. These smart CNDs have a size of around 5 nm and they can be rapidly and largely internalized by the lysosomes of cells, which is proved by the co-location of the florescence of the CNDs and the Lyso Tracker red DND-99 under the supra-resolution microscopy. Owing to the slightly acid microenvironment of lysosomes of cancer cells [33–35], the smart CNDs start surface protonation, making them forming large assemblies (20−30 nm or even larger) in cancer cells. Thus, a red-to-NIR absorption band is produced, which brings high photothermal effect. Since normal cells give a higher pH microenvironment of lysosomes than the cancer ones, these smart CNDs show less aggregation in the lysosomes of normal cells [33,34]. Thus, this therapeutic strategy shows a less side effect toward normal cells during PTT and gives selective therapy to cancer cells. Besides, these unaggregated smart CNDs have a diameter of 4−6 nm, which is reasonably small to penetrate the cell membrane. The in situ formed supra-CNDs give a larger size, which can effectively avoid exocytosis and allows for high photothermal conversation efficiency. Compared with the published work [23], our study achieves the selective and targeting cancer therapy based on the in situ growth of supra-CNDs under the intracellular microenvironments according to the instinct feature of the smart CNDs. No additional targeting ligands were used in present selective treatment strategy, which is different from other PTT-sensitive methods that theselectivity of cancer cells is dominated byantibodies (e.g. PD-1) or other surface ligands [23].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of smart CNDs and supra-CNDs
ce:section.ce:para: The smart CNDs were prepared via a hydrothermal method reported by Huang et al. [36]. Briefly, citric acid (0.07 g) and dicyandiamide (0.25 g) were added in a 50 mL beaker containing 4.0 mL of H2O at 80 °C. Then the solution was heated in a 20 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel at 180 °C for 3 h. The production was centrifuged at 12000 rpm for 15 min and the supernatants were freeze-dried for use.

The achieved smart CNDs solution shows response to pH values. When the pH was adjusted to acid (e.g. 4.7), the CNDs spontaneously aggregated. The yellow smart CNDs solution turned into black after 24 h aging, forming the supra-CNDs. The achieved supra-CNDs were freeze-dried for use.

The aqueous solutions of the supra-CNDs with different concentrations (0, 2.5, 5.0 and 10 mg/mL) in the quartz cuvettes were prepared and they were irradiated by a 650 nm LED (the power density is 407.6 mW/cm2) for 10 min. The temperatures of the CND solutions were recorded with the irradiation time by a thermocouple microprobe (ϕ =0.5 mm) (STPC-510 P, Xiamen Baidewo Technology Co., China) with an accuracy of ± 0.1 °C. The photothermal conversion efficiency (η) of the supra-CNDs is calculated as 42.13 % [37]. Details for calculating η are stated in supporting information.

The cytotoxicity of the smart CNDs for liver cancer cells was assessed by the WST-1 assay kit (Hoffmann-La Roche Ltd). HepG2 cells were cultured in Dulbecco's Modified Eagle Medium (DMEM) which were supplemented with 10 % fatal bovine serum (FBS) at 37 °C under 5 % CO2 for 24 h, then they were added to a 96-well plate (5000 cells per well). The cells were incubated for 12 h and the culture medium was replaced with 260 μL of DMEM containing the smart CNDs at different concentrations (0, 0.8, 1.6, 2.4, 3.2, 4.0, and 4.8 mg/mL, respectively) for another 24 h. Finally, the cells were further incubated for 2 h with WST-1 agent after replacing the CND solution. And the LO2 cells were also treated with the same procedures. The optical density (OD) of the mixture was measured at 450 nm with a Microplate Reader Model (Tecan Sunrise). The cell viabilities were estimated according to the following Eq. (1):
(1) Cell viability [%] = (OD treated/OD control) × 100 %
Here, the control case with the absence of smart CNDs was set. The WST-1 data are provided in supporting information.

To investigate the intracellular uptake and aggregation of smart CNDs, HepG2 cells and LO2 cells were plated into the confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM)-specific dishes (ϕ =25 mm, Corning Inc., New York) and allowed to adhere for 12 h. Then, the cells were divided into two groups: the control and the smart CNDs (2.4 mg/mL) group for 24 h. Then the treated cells were washed 3 times with phosphate buffer solution (PBS) and then imaged by the CLSM (Zeiss LSM780) with 405 nm laser excitation.

For super-resolution imaging, the cells were seeded in Petri dishes for 12 h and then incubated with 2.4 mg/mL smart CNDs for another 24 h. The residual smart CNDs were removed by washing the cells with PBS. Then, the cells were treated with Lyso Tracker red DND-99 for 30 min and washed by PBS again. Finally, the cell fluorescence images were obtained using a DeltaVision OMX imaging system with a 3D Structured Illumination Microscope (SIM) model, which was GE Healthcare (OM06051, Pittsburgh, PA). An analysis software, Imaris X 64 from Bitplane (Zurich, Switzerland), was further used to deal with the image data and obtain the 3D views.

HepG2 cells were plated in a six-well plate for 12 h containing 2.0 mL of Dulbecco’s modified Eagle’s medium supplemented with 10 % FBS for flow apoptosis cytometric evaluation. These cells were then incubated with smart CNDs for 24 h and then washed three times with PBS. Then, the cells were treated with laser irradiation for 4, 8 and 12 min, respectively. The cells without laser irradiation were used as a control. When the cells were treated by irradiation, there are 2.0 mL of Dulbecco’s modified Eagle’s medium left in the 6 well plate for avoiding the cell injury induced by other factors. The resulting cells were trypsinized, harvested and rinsed with PBS and then stained with the mixture of 2.0 μL of Annexin V-APC and 2.0 μL of 7-AAD for 30 min. The control cells without smart CNDs and irradiation for no staining or only Annexin V-APC, 7-AAD staining were set for the settings of proper voltages and other parameters. Finally, the results were analysed with flow cytometry (Beckman CytoFLEX FCM). Other cells (SGC and MCF-7) were treated in the same way.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterizations of smart CNDs and supra-CNDs
ce:section.ce:para: The smart CNDs were prepared by a developed hydrothermal method [36]. The achieved smart CNDs have a pH-propelled aggregation feature. The pH of prepared smart CNDs was measured as 10.2. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image shows that the smart CNDs (Fig. 1b) have a size of 5.1 ± 1.0 nm (Fig. S2). The UV–vis-NIR spectra of yellow smart CNDs (Fig. 1e) shows an individual absorption band at 338 nm, which is in consistence with previous literatures [28–31]. When the pH of smart CNDs solution decreased and aged at 37 °C for 24 h, they formed nanoaggregates spontaneously (Fig. 1c). Fig. S3 shows the TEM images of the nanoaggregates achieved by adjusting the smart CNDs solutions to different pH values. When the pH of solution decreased to 7.0, the size of smart CNDs increased to 20−30 nm (Fig. S3c). Further decreasing pH would cause larger aggregates with dozens or hundreds nm (Fig. S3d and Fig. S4). With the formation of aggregates, the yellow solution became black (Fig. 1f) and a new and broad absorption band appeared at 450−900 nm. The crystal lattice fringes in Fig. 1d are adjacently located with the crystal lattice fringes of d100 = 0.21 nm [28,29], proving that they are the collectives of smart CNDs, which was called supra-CNDs according Qu’s work [32]. Moreover, the supra-CNDs show weak fluorescence due to the self-quenching effect (Fig. 1g and h) and tend to participate with long-term storage. Table 1 lists the absolute quantum yields of the smart CNDs with the reduction of pH values. It can be observed a decline trend of the quantum yield with the pH decreasing. The stabilities of the synthesized supra-CNDs and the smart CNDs were tested by Uv–vis spectroscopy (Fig. S5). There were slightly changes of the absorption spectra of smart CNDs synthetized two weeks ago in contrast to the newly synthesized ones, indicating the enough stability of the smart CNDs for further use. The synthesized supra-CNDs were further manifested by adjusting the pH of solution from 4 to 9 and aging for 12 h, to test whether the pH-propelled self-aggregation is reversible. Fig. S6 shows that the formed supra-CNDs could maintain strong absorption in the range of 450−900 nm when the pH of solution restored from acid to base (from 4.0 to 9.0), confirming that the pH-propelled self-aggregation feature of the smart CNDs is an irreversible process. Thus, once the supra-CNDs forms, they can keep their morphology and property under intracellular environments or culture mediums (pH = 7.2∼7.4).

To further understand the aggregation mechanism of these smart CNDs, we measured the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and infrared spectra of the smart CNDs and supra-CNDs to disclose their surface functional groups. As shown in Fig. 2 a, the absorption bands of (OH) and (NH) at 3100 to 3500 cm−1 and of (CN) at 2154 to 2198 cm−1. Bands from 1550 to 1750 cm−1 are (CO), (CN) and (NH). The 1397 cm−1 band is attributed to (CN) [32]. The XPS spectra of supra-CNDs (Fig. 2b–d) indicate the presence of carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen. The same elements for the smart CNDs are confirmed (Fig. S7a-c). Fig. 2b is the deconvolution of the high-resolution C1s spectra, which reveals many peaks at 284.6, 285.6, 286.2 287.2 and 288.8 eV, corresponding to CC, C − N/C − O, CN/C = O and O − CO bonding, respectively. The N1s in Fig. 2c discloses the N−(C)3 (400.1 eV) and NH (401.6 eV). The O1s in Fig. 2d represents the CO (531.6 eV) and C − OH/C − O − C (533.0 eV) [32]. The XPS and infrared spectra of these two CNDs show similar spectral profiles, indicating that these two samples possess many same functional groups, such as amino, carboxyl groups and aromatic CN heterocycles. From the XPS result, we can find that, compared with smart CNDs, the CO content of the supra-CNDs increases from 37.5 % to 80.5 %, while the NH content changes from 9.6 % to 14.9 % (Table S1), due to the protonation of the COO- and the NH bonds in an acid environment. The steady conjugated structure of the COO- has been broken in an alkaline environment. Accordingly, the COH/C-O-C exists as a form of the CO. These molecular changes illustrate that apparent surface protonation appears in the smart CNDs once they were in an acid environment, which brings electrostatic attraction to these CNDs and finally forms large assemblies.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of pH response of smart CNDs
ce:section.ce:para: The pH response of the smart CNDs was further optimized. The pH of smart CNDs solution (2.4 mg/mL) was regulated to different values by HCl (from 4.7 to 6.8) for 24 h and the Uv–vis-NIR spectra were recorded. As shown in Fig. 3 a, the intensity of the absorption peak increased with decreasing of pH values. A sudden increase of absorption peak (black line) was observed when the pH was adjusted to 4.7 (Fig. 3a), along with a remarkable colour change in Fig. 3b and c, confirming the birth of the supra-CNDs. Moreover, the fluorescence starts self-quenching completely. The pH 4.7 is considered as a pH-response threshold value for the formation of the supra-CNDs. Interestingly, such a slight acid microenvironment exactly corresponds to the pH of lysosomes in cancer cells [33]. Fig. S8 shows the dynamic process of forming the supra-CNDs. With the time increases, the peak at longer wavelengths becomes stronger, proving that the smart CNDs start to form the supra-CNDs. Moreover, we can observe that forming the supra-assemblies requires several hours, which can match the time scale of the internalization of smart CNDs during the cell culture.

Before these smart CNDs were used for intracellular PTT, we evaluated their photothermal effect by measuring the temperature evolutions of the supra-CNDs aqueous solution with different concentrations (0−10 mg/mL) under continuous irradiation of 650 nm light (407.6 mW/cm2). The temperature of the aqueous dispersions increases with the addition of the supra-CNDs (2.5, 5 and 10 mg/mL) after a 10 min irradiation (Fig. 4 a), while the temperature of pure water (without supra-CNDs) only increases as 10.8 °C. These results demonstrate that the supra-CNDs can rapidly and efficiently convert light energy into heat via the efficient photo-absorption at 650 nm. The heating rates of the supra-CNDs solutions also change with the light power (Fig. 4b). It can be found that the temperature of the supra-CNDs aqueous solution of 5.0 mg/mL increases from 20.0–51.9 °C within 10 min, which is much faster than pure water and the smart CNDs under the same concentration (Fig. 4c). These results show that the assembled smart CNDs (supra-CNDs) display a good photothermal effect. Fig. 4d plots the temperature changes of the supra-CND solutions (obtained from 2.5 mg/mL of the smart CNDs, 1.0 mL) as a function of time under the continuous irradiation of 650 nm light (407.6 mW/cm2) till the temperature reaches a steady state. According to the obtained data (Fig. 4e) and the previous literature [37], the photothermal conversion efficiency of supra-CNDs is calculated as 42.13 %. We speculate that the enhanced UV–vis-NIR absorption of the supra-CNDs is attributable to the light induced-electrons deactivating in a nonradiative way [32]. Such high photothermal conversion efficiency makes these supra-CNDs available for a candidate for a PTT-sensitive agent.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Internalization of smart CNDs in cells
ce:section.ce:para: Before these smart CNDs were used for cell system, the internalization of the smart CNDs in cancer and normal cells was monitored by the confocal fluorescent microscopy due to the luminescence of the smart CNDs. Since the concentrations of the smart CNDs can affect the degree of aggregation effectively, it should be optimized first. Here, the LO2 (a normal liver cell line) and HepG2 (a liver cancer cell line) cells were used for optimization of the CND internalization. As shown in Fig. 5 , the fluorescence intensities both increase with the concentration of the smart CNDs no matter in cancer cells or in normal ones.

It is observed that relatively weaker fluorescence is shown in HepG2 cells in comparison to the LO2 cells under the same smart CNDs concentration, indicating the self-quenching appears in HepG2 cells due to the production of many CND aggregates (supra-CNDs), while few aggregates are found in normal cells. The enlarged confocal fluorescence images in Fig. S9 also proves the different self-quenching degrees in two kinds of cells, which can be explained by a lower lysosome pH of the cancer cells relative to the normal cells, leading to worse aggregation of the smart CNDs [33,34].

The photothermal effect of the smart CNDs in different cell lines were further characterized by the temperature imaging, which can reveal difference in the internalization of the smart CNDs among different cell lines. Two cell lines (HepG2 and LO2) were incubated with same amount of smart CNDs (2.4 mg/mL) for 24 h then irradiated with 650 nm light for 8 min. Then they were collected into Eppendorf tubes, respectively and the image was taken by a thermal imager (the Fluke infrared thermometer). Fig. S10 shows that the temperature of HepG2 cells (left) raises higher than that of LO2 cells (right), directly demonstrating that more supra-CNDs were prouced in cancer cells.

Thus, our designed the smart CNDs are expected to make normal/cancer cells differentiation by their diefferent aggregation state of the internalized smart CNDs. The largest difference in fluorescence brightness between cancer and normal cells was observed when the concentration of smart CNDs was 2.4 mg/mL. We used this smart CNDs concentration for further PTT experiment since it ensures a relatively large cell internalization content of the smart CNDs and provides the maximal difference between cancer and normal cells as well.

To apply these smart CNDs for cell study, their biocompatibility should be considered, which was investigated by the WST-1 assay and the data was shown in Fig. S11. These results show that our smart CNDs have low toxicity to cells, in which 2.4 mg/mL of the dose of smart CNDs is able to achieve 80 % of cell viability which is better than many reported CNDs [38].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Tracking positions of smart CNDs in cells
ce:section.ce:para: To disclose the positions of these smart CNDs, the fluorescent colocalization of the smart CNDs and lysosomes was carried out by the super-resolution fluorescent imaging microscope. Fig. 6 show the fluorescent colocalization images of HepG2 (Fig. 6A) and LO2 cells (Fig. 6B), in which the lysosomes were stained by LysoTracker Red DND-99 (a commercial lysosome dye), and the smart CNDs show blue fluorescence. Fig. 6 a1 and 6b1 are the panorama 3D simulation images of HepG2 and LO2 cells incubated with the smart CNDs (showing as green particles in Fig. 6 a1-a4 , Fig. 6b1-b4 , blue in Fig. 6 a5, a7, b5, b7 ), while the lysosomes showing as red particles (Fig. 6 a1-a4 , Fig. 6 b1-b4 , red in Fig. 6 a6, a7, b6, b7 ). In Fig. 6 a1-4 and 6 b1-4 , the locations of two dyes are overlapped well. Moreover, PCC (Pearson's correlation coefficient) and OLC (Overlap coefficient) of the smart CNDs treated cells are 0.86, 0.87 for HepG2 cells and 0.89, 0.87 for LO2 cells. The intensity correlation plots of the two cells are shown in Fig. 6C and D, both of which ensure that the smart CNDs have been internalized in the lysosomes of two cell lines.

ce:section.ce:section-title: PTT effect with the smart CNDs
ce:section.ce:para:
Based on the above data, we evaluated the PTT efficiency of the smart CNDs for cells by the flow cytometry. First, the HepG2 and LO2 cells were cultured with the smart CNDs (2.4 mg/mL) for 24 h, respectively. Next, the cells were treated with or without laser irradiation for 4, 8 and 12 min, respectively. Then, the cells were stained with the mixture of Annexin V-APC and 7-AAD to distinguish live and dead cells accurately, while the no stained cell, Annexin V-APC or 7-AAD stained cells were measured as controls. As illustrated in Fig. 7
a1 , cells in the lower left quadrant are considered as viable ones, while cells in lower right, upper right and upper left quadrants represent that the cells are in the states of early apoptosis, late apoptosis and necrosis, respectively. There are no obvious cell death observed in both HepG2 and LO2 cells containing the smart CNDs with and without 650 nm light irradiation (Fig. 7 a1 and Fig. 7 b1 ), which indicates low cytotoxicity of the smart CNDs and low photodamage for cells (Fig. 7 c1-3 and d1-3 ). When the cells were exposed to a 650 nm light for 4 min, the survival rates of the HepG2 and LO2 cells are both about 90 %. However, when the irradiation time increases to 8 min, remarkable HepG2 cell death is observed, and the apoptotic amount increases from 13.06 % to 82.41 % (late apoptosis plus necrosis), proving that the smart CNDs produce enough heat to kill cells under the 650 nm irradiation for 8 min. Compared to the cancer ones, the normal cells with the same amount of the smart CNDs show almost no identifiable late apoptosis and necrosis in Fig. 7 b3 (the survival rate is 91.32 %). The above results demonstrate the smart CNDs could distinguish the cancer cells and selectively damage the cancer cells under the 650 nm irradiation.

Continuous irradiation (e.g. 12 min in Fig. S12 and Fig. S13) without the smart CNDs brings significant photodamage to both cell lines. Therefore, 8 min irradiation time is an optimal therapy time for our developed PTT sensitization agent. We also investigated the PTT treatment effects of the smart CNDs on other cancer cells (the human breast cancer MCF-7 cells and human SGC-7901 gastric adenocarcinoma). These two cancer cells can also be effectively destroyed by the smart CNDs-based PTT (Fig. S14 and Fig. S15). Accordingly, the PTT strategy based on our developed novel smart CNDs is applicable for many cancer cells.

In summary, a smart CND with an intelligent response to pH is reported, which can form a collective structure and gives rise to strong absorption in the red-to-NIR wavelengths. After internalization of the smart CNDs in the lysosomes of cancer cells, a lower pH environment in malignant cells leads to a more serious aggregation state of the smart CNDs, which can improve the PTT effect by larger light harvesting effect relative to the normal cancer cells. Our study provides a differentiated PTT strategy for cancer and normal cells based on the dynamic supramolecular assembly of the smart CNDs, which is different from other targeting PTT-sensitive nano agents, which the targeting features are endowed with the surface ligands, e.g. antibodies and targeting peptides. The dynamic response of the smart CNDs to intracellular microenvironment is also a highly concerned drug release mechanism, which can be a multifunctional drug carrier due to imaging, drug loading, tumour-targeting three-in-one. This smart carbon-based material as a novel PTT-enhanced agent is also promising in clinic applications for medical purposes due to their low toxicity. This work is meaningful in finding a new type of CNDs that have the self-assembly feature. Moreover our observation can inspire studies on the size and absorption band regulation of CNDs in peruse to NIR natures.
There are no conflicts to declare.

Xue Zhang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing - original draft. Yanting Shen: Writing - review & editing, Visualization. Shuping Xu: Resources, Writing - review & editing, Visualization, Funding acquisition. Jing Yue: Data curation, Methodology. Qiong Guo: Data curation, Formal analysis. Dianshuai Huang: Data curation, Formal analysis. Bai Yang: Supervision, Investigation. Wei Shi: Resources, Supervision. Chongyang Liang: Resources, Supervision. Weiqing Xu: Resources, Funding acquisition, Supervision.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 21573087, 21573092 and 21873039).
